aliases: Tinola (tinolang manok, chicken tinola, ginger chicken soup)

romanized: Tinola (tinolang manok, chicken tinola, ginger chicken soup)

food_subgroup: Clear chicken ginger broth soup with green papaya (or chayote/sayote) and chili leaves; bone-in chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for tinola specifically. Closest parent concept: chicken soup (FOODON:03315763). Tinola is a culturally specific Filipino preparation not yet represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for tinola. Closest parent category: chicken soup with vegetables (A0170).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for tinola or Filipino chicken ginger soup. Not represented in clinical terminology.

serving.description: 1 bowl (~450g: bone-in chicken ~150g + green papaya or sayote ~120g + chili leaves ~80g + clear broth ~250g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Filipino bowl of tinola weighing approximately 450g, comprising bone-in chicken pieces (~150g), green papaya or chayote (~120g), chili leaves (~80g), and clear ginger-fish sauce broth (~250g), providing approximately 261 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines) supplemented with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for green papaya and chicken components
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Tinola as a composite dish is not listed as a single entry in any approved source. Nutrient values are derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Filipino chicken dishes and USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for individual components (bone-in chicken, green papaya, chili leaves, ginger, fish sauce). Values represent a weighted composite per 100g of the assembled dish at the stated portion ratio. Per-100g values are consistent with the input record which cites FAO/INFOODS SEA as primary authority.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI based on analogous foods: green papaya (GI ~58, University of Sydney GI Database) and non-starchy vegetables with lean chicken protein; composite dish GI estimated at ~45 reflecting predominantly low-GI components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); peer-reviewed literature on mixed chicken-vegetable soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tinola in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~45 is estimated from component analysis: green papaya GI ~58 (minor carbohydrate contributor at ~120g per 450g bowl), chayote GI ~40 (low), chili leaves negligible carbohydrate, bone-in chicken GI ~0, clear broth GI ~0. Weighted composite GI estimated at ~45 (low). GL per 450g serving = (45 × 13.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = ~6.1 (low). Protein content (29g per bowl) further blunts postprandial glucose response.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce (patis): ~1,260mg sodium per 450g bowl (280mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tinola to occasional consumption or request reduced patis preparation; substituting 50% of fish sauce with low-sodium alternatives can reduce sodium to approximately 750–800mg per bowl, and the ginger content (gingerols, 6-shogaol) provides modest ACE-inhibitory activity that may offer a minor antihypertensive benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic load (~6.1 per bowl) and high protein content (29g per bowl) blunting postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tinola is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GL of ~6.1 per 450g bowl, predominantly non-starchy vegetable composition, and high lean protein content (29g) which significantly attenuates postprandial blood glucose rise; green papaya contributes only ~7g available carbohydrate per 120g portion despite its moderate GI of ~58.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.7g/100g; ~3.15g per bowl), high protein, anti-inflammatory ginger compounds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tinola is a heart-friendly soup with low saturated fat (~3.15g per bowl), no trans fat, and anti-inflammatory gingerols and shogaols from ginger that may modestly reduce cardiovascular inflammation; sodium content (~1,260mg per bowl) warrants attention for individuals with concurrent hypertension and CVD, and reduced-patis preparation is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ginger compounds (gingerols, 6-shogaol) via COX-2 inhibition; Vitamin C from green papaya and chili leaves supports collagen synthesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tinola is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; ginger provides COX-2 inhibitory anti-inflammatory activity, Vitamin C (~54mg per bowl from green papaya and chili leaves) supports collagen synthesis and antioxidant defence, and high protein (29g per bowl) supports muscle maintenance in RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tinola (tinolang manok) is the quintessential Filipino chicken ginger soup and is widely regarded as the national soup of the Philippines. It is prepared by simmering bone-in chicken pieces with fresh ginger, garlic, onion, and fish sauce (patis) in water until the chicken is tender, then adding green papaya (papaya na hilaw) or chayote (sayote) and finishing with chili leaves (dahon ng sili) or moringa leaves (malunggay) in the last few minutes of cooking. The result is a clear, aromatic, golden broth with a warm ginger-forward flavour, tender chicken, and lightly cooked vegetables. Tinola has deep cultural roots in pre-colonial Philippines and was famously referenced by national hero José Rizal in his 1887 novel Noli Me Tangere as a symbol of social inequality. Per 450g bowl: approximately 261 kcal, 29g protein, 9.9g fat (3.15g saturated), 13.5g carbohydrate, 3.6g dietary fibre, and ~1,260mg sodium (predominantly from patis). Glycemic index ~45 (low), glycemic load ~6.1 (low). Nutritionally, tinola is one of the healthiest Filipino dishes: high lean protein from bone-in chicken, low saturated fat, meaningful Vitamin C (~54mg per bowl from green papaya and chili leaves), beta-carotene from chili leaves, and bioactive anti-inflammatory compounds (gingerols, 6-shogaol) from ginger. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically suitable for Type 2 Diabetes (low GL), cardiovascular health (low saturated fat, anti-inflammatory), and rheumatoid arthritis (ginger COX-2 inhibition). Sodium is the primary dietary concern for hypertension management.
common_user_queries: is tinola good for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines) as primary source, supplemented by USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for individual components. The dish composition and portion size are well-documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component data, clearly documented as such. All values are internally consistent with the 450g bowl description and per-100g figures.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for tinola as a composite Filipino dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level MUFA/PUFA data would require weighted calculation from individual ingredient entries which was not performed in this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as prepared is not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional tinola preparation; confirmed by FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA component data). Sodium of ~1,260mg per 450g bowl is primarily from fish sauce (patis); individuals with hypertension should request reduced-patis preparation. GI of ~45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tinola exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Vitamin A reported as 48 mcg/100g RAE (from chili leaves beta-carotene and chicken liver contribution in bone-in pieces). This record covers the standard tinola preparation with green papaya; chayote (sayote) variant has similar nutritional profile.
